answer:
Top 3 MongoDB Aggregation Mistakes

Mistake 1: Not Using $match Early in the Pipeline

Running expensive stages ($group, $lookup) on the full collection instead of filtering first.

// ❌ BAD: $group processes ALL documents, then filters
db.sales.aggregate([
  { $group: { _id: '$repId', total: { $sum: '$amount' } } },
  { $match: { total: { $gte: 10000 } } } // filters AFTER grouping all docs
]);

// ✅ GOOD: $match first to reduce documents processed
db.sales.aggregate([
  { $match: { date: { $gte: ISODate('2025-01-01') } } }, // filter first!
  { $group: { _id: '$repId', total: { $sum: '$amount' } } },
  { $match: { total: { $gte: 10000 } } }
]);
// $match at the start can use indexes → 100x faster

Mistake 2: Using $lookup Without Indexes on the Foreign Collection

$lookup (left outer join) performs a collection scan on the foreign collection for each document if there's no index.

// ❌ BAD: $lookup without index on contacts.userId
db.users.aggregate([
  { $lookup: {
    from: 'contacts',
    localField: '_id',
    foreignField: 'userId', // no index on contacts.userId!
    as: 'contacts'
  }}
]);
// For 1000 users × 50000 contacts = 50M comparisons!

// ✅ GOOD: Create index first
db.contacts.createIndex({ userId: 1 });
// Now $lookup uses index scan → fast

Mistake 3: Not Using $project to Limit Fields

Passing all fields through every pipeline stage wastes memory, especially with large documents.

// ❌ BAD: Full documents through entire pipeline
db.contacts.aggregate([
  { $match: { status: 'active' } },
  { $group: { _id: '$city', count: { $sum: 1 } } }
  // Each doc has 20 fields but we only need city!
]);

// ✅ GOOD: Project early to reduce pipeline memory
db.contacts.aggregate([
  { $match: { status: 'active' } },
  { $project: { city: 1, _id: 0 } }, // only keep what you need
  { $group: { _id: '$city', count: { $sum: 1 } } }
]);

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard (MongoDB), the sales pipeline aggregation grouped deals by stage and calculated totals. Initially, it processed 50K deals without a $match filter — taking 4 seconds. Adding $match: { createdAt: { $gte: startOfMonth } } at the start reduced processed documents from 50K to 3K, bringing response time to 200ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using allowDiskUse: true as a fix for slow aggregations instead of optimizing the pipeline. It masks the real problem — too much data being processed.

Mitigation: Fix the pipeline first ($match early, $project to reduce fields, add indexes). Only use allowDiskUse for legitimate large-dataset analytics that can't fit in the 100MB memory limit.

Definition

MongoDB Aggregation is a pipeline framework for transforming and analyzing data within MongoDB. Each stage in the pipeline ($match, $group, $project, $sort, $lookup) transforms the data, passing results to the next stage. It's MongoDB's equivalent of SQL's GROUP BY, JOIN, and subqueries.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the aggregation pipeline powers the sales analytics: grouping deals by stage (lead → qualified → proposal → closed), calculating conversion rates, and ranking sales reps by revenue — all in a single database query instead of fetching all data and processing in Node.js.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Sales pipeline analytics — Mongoose aggregation
const salesReport = await Sale.aggregate([
  // Stage 1: Filter to current quarter
  { $match: {
    createdAt: { $gte: new Date('2025-01-01') },
    status: { $ne: 'deleted' }
  }},

  // Stage 2: Group by sales rep
  { $group: {
    _id: '$repId',
    totalRevenue: { $sum: '$amount' },
    dealCount: { $sum: 1 },
    avgDeal: { $avg: '$amount' },
    maxDeal: { $max: '$amount' }
  }},

  // Stage 3: Enrich with rep details
  { $lookup: {
    from: 'users',
    localField: '_id',
    foreignField: '_id',
    as: 'rep'
  }},
  { $unwind: '$rep' },

  // Stage 4: Format output
  { $project: {
    repName: '$rep.name',
    totalRevenue: 1,
    dealCount: 1,
    avgDeal: { $round: ['$avgDeal', 2] }
  }},

  // Stage 5: Top 10 by revenue
  { $sort: { totalRevenue: -1 } },
  { $limit: 10 }
]);

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Building complex aggregation pipelines in production code without explain(). A 10-stage pipeline might scan the entire collection if the $match stage doesn't use an index.

Mitigation: Use .explain('executionStats') on aggregation pipelines to verify index usage. Ensure the first $match stage uses an indexed field.

Definition

Mongoose Modeling is the practice of defining MongoDB document schemas, relationships, and middleware using Mongoose ODM (Object Document Mapper). It adds a structured, type-safe layer on top of MongoDB's schema-less nature — defining fields, types, validations, virtuals, and hooks in JavaScript/TypeScript.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard (MongoDB/Mongoose), the Lead model defines required fields (company, email, status), validation rules (email format, status enum), and pre-save hooks (normalize email to lowercase, update lastModified). This prevents invalid leads from entering the database — catching errors at the application layer before they reach MongoDB.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Mongoose schema for CRM contacts
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const contactSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: {
    type: String,
    required: [true, 'Name is required'],
    trim: true,
    maxlength: [100, 'Name must be under 100 characters']
  },
  email: {
    type: String,
    required: true,
    unique: true,
    lowercase: true,
    match: [/^\S+@\S+\.\S+$/, 'Invalid email format']
  },
  phone: String,
  company: String,
  status: {
    type: String,
    enum: ['active', 'inactive', 'lead'],
    default: 'lead'
  },
  tags: [String],
  avatar: String,
  createdBy: {
    type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId,
    ref: 'User',
    required: true
  }
}, {
  timestamps: true, // auto adds createdAt, updatedAt
  toJSON: { virtuals: true }
});

// Index for common queries
contactSchema.index({ email: 1 });
contactSchema.index({ status: 1, createdAt: -1 });
contactSchema.index({ tags: 1 });

// Virtual: full display name
contactSchema.virtual('displayName').get(function() {
  return this.company ? `${this.name} (${this.company})` : this.name;
});

// Pre-save hook: normalize data
contactSchema.pre('save', function(next) {
  if (this.isModified('name')) {
    this.name = this.name.replace(/\s+/g, ' ').trim();
  }
  next();
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('Contact', contactSchema);

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Not adding indexes in the Mongoose schema. MongoDB only creates an index on _id by default — queries on other fields do collection scans.

Mitigation: Add schema.index() for every field used in find(), sort(), or aggregate $match. Use mongoose.set('debug', true) in development to see actual queries and spot missing indexes.

Definition

Mongoose Modeling solves the schema-less chaos problem in MongoDB applications. Without Mongoose, any document shape can be inserted — typos in field names, wrong data types, missing required fields. Mongoose adds a validation layer that catches these issues at the application level before they reach the database.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub (MongoDB/Mongoose), the contact model enforces: email uniqueness (prevents duplicates during bulk CSV upload of 500 contacts), required fields (name, email), and custom validation (phone number format). When the bulk upload processes a CSV row with a duplicate email, Mongoose throws a 11000 duplicate key error that the controller catches and reports as "Row 42: Email already exists" — instead of silently creating a duplicate.

Problems Solved

  • Data consistency: Schema validation ensures every document has the expected shape
  • Type safety: String fields reject numbers, date fields reject strings
  • Business rules: Custom validators enforce domain rules (email format, phone format, enum values)
  • Relationship integrity: Populate() provides JOIN-like functionality for referenced documents

Visual Guide

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, users reported seeing "undefined" in the contact list. Investigation revealed that some contacts were inserted via the API with firstName instead of name — MongoDB accepted them because it's schema-less. Adding strict: true (default) to the Mongoose schema rejected unknown fields, and adding required: true on the name field prevented future occurrences. A migration script cleaned up the 47 malformed documents.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using Schema.Types.Mixed or strict: false to "be flexible." This defeats the purpose of Mongoose and allows arbitrary data in.

Mitigation: Keep strict: true (default). For genuinely dynamic fields, use a dedicated metadata field of type Map instead of making the entire schema flexible.

Definition

Data Partitioning is the practice of splitting a large dataset across multiple storage units (tables, collections, or servers) to improve query performance and manageability. There are two main types: horizontal partitioning (sharding — splitting rows) and vertical partitioning (splitting columns into separate tables).

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, as the company grew to 5,000+ employees with 500K+ leave records, the leaves table became slow for date-range queries. Partitioning by year (leaves_2024, leaves_2025) means queries for "leaves this month" only scan the current year's partition — 50K rows instead of 500K.

Visual Guide

Code Example

-- PostgreSQL Range Partitioning by year
CREATE TABLE leaves (
  id         SERIAL,
  employee_id INT NOT NULL,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  end_date   DATE NOT NULL,
  status     TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
) PARTITION BY RANGE (start_date);

-- Create yearly partitions
CREATE TABLE leaves_2024 PARTITION OF leaves
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2025-01-01');

CREATE TABLE leaves_2025 PARTITION OF leaves
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-01-01') TO ('2026-01-01');

-- Query automatically targets correct partition
SELECT * FROM leaves
WHERE start_date BETWEEN '2025-03-01' AND '2025-03-31';
-- PostgreSQL only scans leaves_2025, skips leaves_2024

-- Verify partition pruning with EXPLAIN
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM leaves WHERE start_date = '2025-06-15';
-- Should show: Scan on leaves_2025 (not the parent table)

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Choosing the wrong partition key. Partitioning by employee_id doesn't help date-range queries — the query still scans all partitions.

Mitigation: Partition on the column most frequently used in WHERE clauses. For time-series data (leaves, logs, orders), partition by date. For multi-tenant apps, partition by tenant_id.

Definition

Data Partitioning solves the large table performance problem. When a table grows beyond millions of rows, queries slow down, indexes become huge, and maintenance operations (VACUUM, REINDEX) take hours. Partitioning splits the table into smaller, manageable pieces that PostgreSQL can query independently.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the sales records table grew to 2M rows. Monthly reports queried WHERE date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31', scanning the entire table. After monthly partitioning, the same query only scanned 1 partition (100K rows) — a 20× speedup. Old partitions could also be archived to cold storage without affecting active queries.

Problems Solved

  • Query performance: Partition pruning skips irrelevant data — scans only matching partitions
  • Maintenance: VACUUM and REINDEX operate on smaller partitions, reducing lock time
  • Data lifecycle: Drop old partitions instantly instead of slow DELETE operations
  • Parallel queries: PostgreSQL can scan multiple partitions in parallel

Code Example

// MongoDB: Sharding for horizontal partitioning
// In ContactsHub, shard contacts by userId for multi-tenant isolation

// 1. Enable sharding on database
sh.enableSharding('contactshub');

// 2. Create shard key index
db.contacts.createIndex({ userId: 1, _id: 1 });

// 3. Shard the collection
sh.shardCollection('contactshub.contacts', { userId: 1 });

// Now queries with userId filter go to one shard only:
db.contacts.find({ userId: 'user123' }); // targets single shard

// In Mongoose:
const Contact = mongoose.model('Contact', contactSchema);
const userContacts = await Contact.find({ userId: req.user.id })
  .sort({ createdAt: -1 })
  .limit(50);
// With sharding, this hits only the shard containing this user's data

// PostgreSQL: List partitioning for status-based access
CREATE TABLE leaves (
  id SERIAL, employee_id INT, status TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP
) PARTITION BY LIST (status);

CREATE TABLE leaves_active PARTITION OF leaves
  FOR VALUES IN ('pending', 'approved');
CREATE TABLE leaves_archived PARTITION OF leaves
  FOR VALUES IN ('rejected', 'cancelled', 'expired');
-- Active queries only scan leaves_active

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Partitioning too early. Tables under 1M rows rarely benefit from partitioning — the overhead of managing partitions outweighs the performance gain.

Mitigation: Partition when: table exceeds 1M rows, queries consistently scan more data than needed, or maintenance windows are too long. Start with simple range partitioning by date — it covers 80% of use cases.
